methods.method_name: Ice Core Data Acquisition and Analysis
methods.description: This method describes the process of obtaining ice core samples from various Antarctic sites (EDC, EDML, TALDICE, Vostok) and analyzing them for calcium (Ca2+) and sodium (Na+) concentrations using Continuous Flow Analysis (CFA) for high-resolution data and Ion Chromatography (IC) for coarse-resolution or specific intervals. It includes details on measurement parameters, resolution, detection limits, and data characteristics.

steps.description: This study is based on high-resolution calcium (Ca 2+ ) and sodium (Na + ) CFA data from the Antarctic ice cores EDC (Röthlisberger et al., 2004;Bigler et al., 2006Bigler et al., , 2010)), EDML (Ca 2+ CFA data have already been discussed in Kaufmann et al., 2010; Na + CFA data represent new data), and TALDICE (new data).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain high-resolution ice core samples from EDC, EDML, and TALDICE sites.

steps.description: Besides these high-resolution data, we included Na + and Ca 2+ records from the Vostok ice core in our multi-site comparison, which are available in coarse resolution only (De Angelis et al., 1997) and are based on discrete ion chromatographic (IC) analysis of samples after manual decontamination.
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain coarse-resolution ice core samples from Vostok site.

steps.description: All analyses were performed using CFA (Röthlisberger et al., 2000;Kaufmann et al., 2008), providing aerosol chemistry data in unprecedented resolution and avoiding analytical blank issues, which are especially important in the case of Ca 2+ (Fischer et al., 2007a;Kaufmann et al., 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Continuous Flow Analysis (CFA) on high-resolution ice core samples to measure Ca2+ and Na+ concentrations.
inputs: High-resolution ice core samples (EDC, EDML, TALDICE)
outputs: High-resolution Ca2+ and Na+ concentration data (raw)

steps.description: CFA measurements of TALDICE were performed in the years 2006-2008 at the Alfred-Wegener-Institut (AWI) in Bremerhaven, Germany.The covered depth interval (305-1435 m) corresponds to an age interval of 3.8-150 ka BP.Due to signal dispersion in the CFA system the effective depth resolution is 1 cm (Ca 2+ ) and 2 cm (Na + ) at a melt speed of approx.4 cm min −1 as it was applied during the TALDICE analyses (Kaufmann et al., 2008).Limit of detections (LOD) are 0.1 ppbw for Ca 2+ and 0.5 ppbw for Na + , respectively, measurement errors are estimated to be generally below 10 % (Röthlisberger et al., 2000;Kaufmann et al., 2008).
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct CFA measurements for TALDICE ice core samples with specified depth/age intervals, melt speed, and note effective resolution, LOD, and errors.
outputs: TALDICE Ca2+ and Na+ concentration data (raw)
Measurement parameters (melt speed, resolution, LOD, errors)

steps.description: Major gaps in the new TALDICE data sets can be found in the Ca 2+ record from 490-502 m and from 535-578 m, as well as in the Na + record from 441-449 m, respectively.Apart from the mentioned gaps, no gaps longer than five consecutive metres can be found in either of the new records (see Fig. 2).The longer gaps are due to maintenance and failure of the respective CFA detector, while measurements where continued.Other smaller gaps are mainly due to bad ice quality (mostly in the brittle ice zone) or distinct visible (ash) layers which were not processed with CFA.
steps.searchable_summary: Note and document data gaps occurring during CFA measurements due to maintenance, detector failure, bad ice quality, or distinct visible layers.

steps.description: EDML Na + data in the age interval 123.3-132.3ka BP (depth: 2341-2383 m) have been analysed using IC (Fischer et al., 2007a) in 1 m resolution due to missing CFA Na + data in this depth interval.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze specific EDML Na+ data interval using Ion Chromatography (IC) in 1m resolution.
outputs: EDML Na+ concentration data (raw, specific interval)

steps.description: On the Vostok ice core 237 discontinuous measurements have been analysed with a mean depth resolution of 10.1 m using IC only (De Angelis et al., 1997).Each individual Vostok ice sample (once decontaminated by rinsing) was generally 10 cm long.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform discontinuous Ion Chromatography (IC) measurements on Vostok ice core samples after manual decontamination.
outputs: Vostok Ca2+ and Na+ concentration data (raw, discontinuous)

methods.method_name: Ice Core Data Processing and Dating
methods.description: This method details the procedures for processing raw ice core concentration data, assigning ages based on established age scales, interpolating data, calculating averages (medians) over specific time intervals, and handling missing data to create standardized time series for comparison.

steps.description: In order to compare the different ice cores, the different resolutions of the data sets have to be considered.Therefore, equidistant age averages (100 and 500 yr for all the high-resolution records, and 500 yr only for the Vostok records) were calculated for all four ice cores using the well-synchronised recent AICC2012 age scales (Bazin et al., 2013;Veres et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Load raw high-resolution ice core data (EDC, EDML, TALDICE) and coarse-resolution data (Vostok) with associated depth information.
inputs: Raw CFA/IC data files (from Data Acquisition method)

steps.description: equidistant age averages (100 and 500 yr for all the high-resolution records, and 500 yr only for the Vostok records) were calculated for all four ice cores using the well-synchronised recent AICC2012 age scales (Bazin et al., 2013;Veres et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Load established ice core age scale data (AICC2012).

steps.description: The age scale (given in 1-3 m resolution, depending on the ice core and depth range) has been interpolated to assign an age to each individual data point of the high-resolution records.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpolate the age scale to assign an age to each individual data point of the high-resolution records based on its depth.

steps.description: In the same way the accumulation rates have been interpolated in order to calculate deposition fluxes and to reconstruct the atmospheric signals (see next section).
steps.searchable_summary: Interpolate accumulation rates for high-resolution records based on depth to prepare for deposition flux calculations.
outputs: High-resolution data with interpolated accumulation rates

steps.description: Centennial medians have then been calculated from the highresolution data on the common age scale for each record.Medians have been calculated to prevent the hundred year mean values from being biased by single extreme events.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate centennial (100 yr) medians from the high-resolution data points falling within each 100-year interval on the common age scale.
outputs: 100-year median concentration values for high-resolution records

steps.description: The same threshold has been applied for the calculation of the 500 yr medians.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate 500 yr medians from the high-resolution data points falling within each 500-year interval on the common age scale.
outputs: 500-year median concentration values for high-resolution records

steps.description: If more than two thirds of the high-resolution data within the interval, from which a 100 yr median has been calculated, were missing, the median has been discarded, again in order to prevent an extreme event to bias the result significantly.The same threshold has been applied for the calculation of the 500 yr medians.With this threshold we discarded up to 7 % of the calculated data of a single record.
steps.searchable_summary: Discard calculated medians (100 yr or 500 yr) if more than two thirds of the high-resolution data points within that interval were missing.

steps.description: For the Vostok records, available in coarser resolution only, we simply interpolated the age scale to assign an age to each data point at its individual depth.
steps.searchable_summary: For Vostok records, interpolate the age scale to assign an age to each data point based on its individual depth.

steps.description: From these data points we then interpolated Na + and Ca 2+ concentrations to obtain a value for each 500 yr step in accordance with the 500 yr means of the three other cores.No 100 yr values have been calculated for Vostok due to the coarse resolution of the original data sets.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpolate Na+ and Ca2+ concentrations for Vostok data points to obtain a concentration value for each 500 yr step on the common age scale.

methods.description: This method involves applying a simple exponential model to describe atmospheric aerosol transport between ice core sites and verifying its performance by comparing relationships derived from the model (using climate model outputs) with relationships observed in the processed ice core data.

steps.description: Assuming a simple model of atmospheric aerosol transport as described by Fischer et al. (2007a) with an aerosol concentration C air (0) at the source and an exponential loss during transport, the concentration of the aerosol in the air parcel at any time t can be described as where τ is the average atmospheric residence time of the aerosol.
steps.searchable_summary: Define the simple exponential transport model equation relating concentration, source concentration, time, and residence time.

steps.description: Using this simple transport model and assuming a single source region (e.g.Patagonia), the logarithmic air concentration of the aerosol at one site (e.g.EDC) with transport time t 2 from the source location to the site can be expressed by the concentration at another site (e.g.EDML) with transport time t 1 according to ln [C air (t 2 )] = ln [C air (t 1 )] − t/τ, where t = t 2 − t 1 is the difference of the transport times from the source to the two sites, assuming that the atmospheric residence time along the two transport ways is the same.Thus the relation of the logarithms of the atmospheric aerosol concentration at the two drilling sites is linear to first order with an offset t/τ .
steps.searchable_summary: Derive the relationship between the logarithm of atmospheric aerosol concentration at two sites based on the transport model.

steps.description: In order to verify our transport model, we consider climate model simulations described in Albani et al. (2012b) and Mahowald et al. (2006) ... For the model results, we consider modelled dust deposition flux and dust load at the drilling sites.
steps.searchable_summary: Load climate model simulation outputs for dust deposition flux and dust load at the ice core drilling sites for relevant climate periods.
inputs: Climate model output files (dust flux, dust load)
outputs: Climate model data for ice core locations (dust flux, dust load)

steps.description: A version of the model with the best accuracy in reproducing ice core deposition (and source apportionment) is the 1 yr tuned case from Albani et al. (2012b).Tuning was halving Australian emissions for current climate, and doubling it for the LGM, compared to a companion couple of runs spanning 10 years.
steps.searchable_summary: Select or configure a specific tuned version of the climate model simulation (e.g., 1 yr tuned case) that includes adjustments to source emissions.

steps.description: For the exponential transport model, we considered the flux of ice core aerosol proxy data representative of the atmospheric aerosol concentration at the drill sites (see Sect. 2.3).
steps.searchable_summary: Load processed ice core aerosol proxy data (flux or concentration) from different sites for comparison with model results.
inputs: Processed ice core data (e.g., 500-year averages from Data Processing method)
outputs: Ice core data for comparison (flux or concentration)

steps.description: A very similar behaviour can be observed when comparing the model results in Fig. 7a with the ice core data in Fig. 6. ... Based on the overall good correspondence of the slopes of the regression lines for atmospheric dust loadings in Fig. 7a and c, we conclude that our transport model appears to be able to describe the transport effect on dust concentrations in different regions on the East Antarctic ice sheet.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare the relationships (e.g., linear regressions of log concentrations/fluxes) between pairs of ice core sites using the climate model outputs and the processed ice core data.
inputs: Climate model data for ice core locations
Processed ice core data for comparison

steps.description: If we now consider dust deposition instead of atmospheric surface concentration of the very same model run (Fig. 7b), we see all the regression lines having slopes close to one, but more distinct offsets. ... Using the 10 yr runs results in slightly more scattered slopes for both dust load (Fig. 7c) and deposition flux (Fig. 7d), but allows for an estimation of the error and r 2 of the regression lines (in Table 3 the slopes, standard deviations, and r 2 values for all regressions shown in Fig. 7 are indicated).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform linear regression analysis on the climate model outputs (dust load and deposition flux) for pairs of sites across different climate periods (e.g., Holocene and LGM).
inputs: Climate model data for site pairs (dust load, dust flux)
outputs: Regression parameters (slope, offset, r^2) for model data

steps.description: Using the 10 yr runs results in slightly more scattered slopes for both dust load (Fig. 7c) and deposition flux (Fig. 7d), but allows for an estimation of the error and r 2 of the regression lines (in Table 3 the slopes, standard deviations, and r 2 values for all regressions shown in Fig. 7 are indicated).
steps.searchable_summary: For multi-year climate model simulations (e.g., 10 yr runs), calculate statistical measures like standard deviation and r^2 for the regression lines to estimate uncertainty.
inputs: Regression results from multi-year climate model simulations

steps.description: Based on the overall good correspondence of the slopes of the regression lines for atmospheric dust loadings in Fig. 7a and c, we conclude that our transport model appears to be able to describe the transport effect on dust concentrations in different regions on the East Antarctic ice sheet.
steps.searchable_summary: Evaluate the correspondence between the slopes and offsets of the regression lines obtained from the climate model outputs and those observed in the processed ice core data to assess the transport model's ability.
